hook: You already know how to work with vectors in the standard basis of $\mathbb{R}^n$, where coordinates are just the usual components. But now suppose you have a basis $\mathcal{B} = \{\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2\}$ that is tilted or scaled differently from the standard axes — vectors look completely different when described relative to $\mathcal{B}$. If someone hands you a vector written in $\mathcal{B}$-coordinates, can you convert it back to standard coordinates, and vice versa?
concept: Every vector in a finite-dimensional vector space can be written uniquely as a linear combination of any basis. The coefficients in that combination are the coordinates of the vector in that basis. Formally, if $\mathcal{B} = \{\mathbf{b}_1, \dots, \mathbf{b}_n\}$ is a basis for $V$ and $\mathbf{v} = c_1\mathbf{b}_1 + \cdots + c_n\mathbf{b}_n$, then $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}} = (c_1, \dots, c_n)$ is the coordinate vector of $\mathbf{v}$ relative to $\mathcal{B}$.
anatomy: The key objects in a change-of-basis problem are: the old basis $\mathcal{B} = \{\mathbf{b}_1, \dots, \mathbf{b}_n\}$ and the new basis $\mathcal{C} = \{\mathbf{c}_1, \dots, \mathbf{c}_n\}$; the change-of-basis matrix $P_{\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{C}}$, whose columns are the $\mathcal{C}$-coordinates of each $\mathcal{B}$ basis vector; the coordinate vector $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}}$ in the old basis; and the resulting coordinate vector $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{C}} = P_{\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{C}}\,[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}}$ in the new basis. The inverse matrix $P_{\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{C}}^{-1}$ reverses the conversion.

worked_example.description_latex: We are given a non-standard basis $\mathcal{B}$ for $\mathbb{R}^2$ and a vector $\mathbf{v}$ written in standard coordinates. Our goal is to find $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}}$, the coordinate vector of $\mathbf{v}$ relative to $\mathcal{B}$, so we need to write $\mathbf{v}$ as a linear combination $c_1 \mathbf{b}_1 + c_2 \mathbf{b}_2$.

We rewrite the equation $c_1\mathbf{b}_1 + c_2\mathbf{b}_2 = \mathbf{v}$ as a matrix equation by placing the basis vectors as columns of a matrix $P$. Solving this system will give us the coordinates $(c_1, c_2)$ directly.

We compute $P^{-1}$, the inverse of the matrix whose columns are the basis vectors, because multiplying both sides by $P^{-1}$ isolates the coordinate vector. For a $2\times 2$ matrix $\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}$, the inverse is $\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{pmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{pmatrix}$, and here $\det(P) = (1)(-1)-(1)(1) = -2$.

Multiplying $P^{-1}$ by $\mathbf{v}$ gives the $\mathcal{B}$-coordinate vector. This result tells us that $\mathbf{v} = 2\mathbf{b}_1 + 1\cdot\mathbf{b}_2$, which you can verify by computing $2\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix} + 1\cdot\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}3\\1\end{pmatrix}$ — exactly $\mathbf{v}$.

partial_example.description_latex: We want to find $[\mathbf{w}]_{\mathcal{B}}$. Begin by setting up the matrix $P$ whose columns are the basis vectors, so that $P\,[\mathbf{w}]_{\mathcal{B}} = \mathbf{w}$.
Because $P$ is diagonal, its inverse is found by taking reciprocals of the diagonal entries. We now have $P^{-1}$ ready to apply to $\mathbf{w}$.

Now compute $[\mathbf{w}]_{\mathcal{B}} = P^{-1}\mathbf{w}$ by multiplying $\begin{pmatrix}1/2 & 0\\0 & 1/3\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}6\\-3\end{pmatrix}$. What is the coordinate vector of $\mathbf{w}$ relative to $\mathcal{B}$?

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $\mathcal{B} = \left\{\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix}0\\1\end{pmatrix}\right\}$ be the standard basis of $\mathbb{R}^2$ and $\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix}4\\-7\end{pmatrix}$. Find $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}}$.

Suppose $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{pmatrix}3\\-1\end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathcal{B} = \left\{\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix}0\\2\end{pmatrix}\right\}$. Find the standard-coordinate vector $\mathbf{v}$.

Let $\mathcal{B} = \left\{\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix}\right\}$ and $\mathcal{C} = \left\{\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix}0\\1\end{pmatrix}\right\}$ (the standard basis). Given $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{pmatrix}2\\1\end{pmatrix}$, find $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{C}}$ using the change-of-basis matrix $P_{\mathcal{B}\to\mathcal{C}}$.

A signal processing system stores vectors in the basis $\mathcal{B} = \left\{\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix}\right\}$. An engineer records a measurement as $[\mathbf{s}]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{pmatrix}5\\2\end{pmatrix}$. What is the standard-coordinate vector $\mathbf{s}$ that a classical instrument would display?

common_mistakes: Students often place basis vectors as rows instead of columns when building the matrix $P$, which produces the transpose of the intended matrix and yields completely wrong coordinates. Always form $P$ by making each basis vector a column.

When converting from $\mathcal{B}$-coordinates to standard coordinates, students sometimes multiply $P^{-1}\mathbf{v}$ instead of $P\,[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}}$, inverting the direction of the transformation. Remember: to go from $\mathcal{B}$-coordinates to standard, multiply by $P$ (not $P^{-1}$); to go from standard to $\mathcal{B}$-coordinates, multiply by $P^{-1}$.

Students confuse which matrix is the change-of-basis matrix when converting between two non-standard bases $\mathcal{B}$ and $\mathcal{C}$, forgetting that $P_{\mathcal{B}\to\mathcal{C}}$ has columns equal to the $\mathcal{C}$-coordinates of the $\mathcal{B}$ vectors, not the other way around. Drawing an arrow from $\mathcal{B}$ to $\mathcal{C}$ and labeling the columns explicitly helps avoid this reversal.
untested_variants: Change of basis for a linear transformation: finding the matrix representation $[T]_{\mathcal{C}}$ of a linear map $T$ after a change of basis, using the formula $[T]_{\mathcal{C}} = P^{-1}[T]_{\mathcal{B}}P$.
Coordinates in vector spaces other than $\mathbb{R}^n$, such as finding the coordinate vector of a polynomial $p(x) = 1 + 2x + x^2$ relative to a non-standard basis of $\mathcal{P}_2$.
Change of basis in $\mathbb{R}^3$ involving a $3\times 3$ change-of-basis matrix, where inverting requires row reduction rather than the simple $2\times 2$ formula.
Orthonormal bases and the simplification that arises when $\mathcal{B}$ is orthonormal, so that $P^{-1} = P^T$ and coordinates can be found by dot products.
